9: arkei soi he charis mou he gar dynamis en astheneia teleitai (arkeî soi hē charis mou, hē gar dynamis en astheneia teleitai): 'My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness.' This is the only recorded direct speech of the risen Christ to Paul outside the Damascus road and Acts (cf. 2 Corinthians 18:9-10 for a similar vision-word). Teleitai (is brought to completion/perfection) — the power reaches its telos when weakness provides no competition. skolops te sarki (skólops tē sarki, 'thorn in the flesh'): skolops means a stake or pointed object — more severe than 'thorn'; the 'messenger of Satan' (angelos satana) frames it as a demonically-mediated affliction that God uses for Paul's sanctification.
1: I must go on boasting (kauchasthai dei) — the dei (must, it is necessary) frames boasting as a compulsory rhetorical act, not voluntary self-promotion. Though there is nothing to be gained by it, I will go on to visions and revelations (optasias kai apokalypseis) of the Lord.
2: I know a man in Christ (anthrōpon en Christō) who fourteen years ago was caught up (harpagenta, snatched up) to the third heaven — the distancing in the third person (a man in Christ rather than I myself) is Paul's way of boasting while maintaining the posture of the one who will only boast in weakness. Harpazō (snatch up) is used for the violent divine seizure of Elijah (LXX 2 Kings 2:11) and the eschatological rapture (1 Thessalonians 4:17).
3: And I know that this man — whether in the body or out of the body I do not know, God knows — the double disclaimer (2 Corinthians 12:2 and 3) maintains epistemological humility about the mode of the vision while affirming its reality.
4: Was caught up into paradise (eis ton paradeison) — paradeisos (paradise, from Persian pairidaeza, walled garden) was used in the LXX for the garden of Eden (Genesis 2:8) and in Jewish apocalyptic for the heavenly garden where the righteous await resurrection (1 Enoch 2 Corinthians 60:8; 4 Ezra 7:36). He heard things that cannot be told (arrhēta rhēmata), which man is not permitted to utter.
5: On behalf of this man I will boast, but on my own behalf I will not boast, except of my weaknesses — the grammatical distinction between the heavenly-vision man (third person) and the boasting-in-weakness Paul (first person) is maintained throughout. The split is rhetorical and reveals Paul's discomfort with mystical self-disclosure.
6: Though if I wish to boast, I would not be a fool (aphrōn), for I would be speaking the truth — unlike the conventional fool's-speech disclaimer (I speak as a fool), here Paul notes that the visionary boast would actually be true; his restraint is therefore strategic, not epistemic.
7: To keep me from being too elated (hyperairōmai, lifted up, exalted beyond measure) by the surpassing greatness (hyperbolē) of the revelations, a thorn (skolops) was given me in the flesh — skolops means a stake or thorn; medical interpreters have proposed epilepsy, eye disease, or malarial fever. A messenger (angelos) of Satan to harass me (hina me kolaphizē, to strike with the fist, beat) — the same verb used for striking Jesus in the Passion (Matthew 26:67).
8: Three times I pleaded (parekalesa) with the Lord about this, that it should leave me — parekaleo (beg, entreat) is the same root as paraklēsis (encouragement, comfort) that opens the letter (2 Corinthians 1:3-7). Paul's prayer for removal of the thorn is the inverse of his own teaching: the God of all paraklēsis comforts in all affliction.
10: For the sake of Christ, then, I am content (eudokō, I take pleasure, am well pleased) with weaknesses, insults (hybresin), hardships (anankais, necessities, straits), persecutions (diōgmois), and calamities (stenochoriais, tight places, distresses) — the five nouns form a compressed version of the peristasis catalogue from chapter 11. For when I am weak (astheneō), then I am strong (dynatos).
11: I have been a fool! You forced me to it (hymeis me ēnagkasate) — the aorist indicates a completed action: the Corinthians' failure to commend Paul is what required the fool's speech. For I ought to have been commended by you, since I was not at all inferior (hysterēsa, lacking, falling short) to these super-apostles (tōn hyperulian apostolōn), even though I am nothing.
12: The signs of a true apostle were performed among you with utmost patience (en pasē hypomonē) — hypomonē (patient endurance) is not passive resignation but active perseverance. Signs and wonders (sēmeiois kai terasin) and mighty works (dynamesi) — the three-term formula for miraculous attestation echoes Deuteronomy 34:11.
13: For in what were you less favored than the rest of the churches, except that I myself did not burden you? — the question is designed to sting: the only way the Corinthians were treated less well was that Paul refused to take their money. Forgive me this wrong! (charissasthe moi tēn adikian tautēn) — the mock apology is barbed irony.
14: Here for the third time I am ready to come to you — the announcement of the third visit frames everything from 12:14 to 2 Corinthians 13:10. And I will not be a burden (ou katanarkanō), for I seek not what is yours but you (ou ta hyma alla hymas) — the distinction between possessing the community's goods and possessing the community itself is the key to Paul's pastoral ethic.
15: I will most gladly spend (dapanēsō) and be spent (ekdapanēthēsomai) for your souls — the intensification from spending money to spending the self. The double compound dapanēsō/ekdapanēthēsomai (spend/spend out completely) maps the logic of apostolic self-giving: first resources, then the person.
16: But granting that I myself did not burden you, I was crafty (hyparchōn panourgos, being a scoundrel) — panourgia (craftiness) appeared in 11:3 for the serpent's cunning; now the Corinthians apparently use it against Paul. He quotes their accusation ironically: yes, and caught you by deceit (dolō hymas elabon).
17: Did I take advantage of you through any of those whom I sent to you? — the question tests the consistency of Paul's practice: if he was crafty, his agents should show the same craftiness. The logic works in reverse — if Titus and the others acted with integrity, the accusation of Paul's craftiness collapses.
18: I urged (parekalesa) Titus to go and sent the brother with him — the parallel construction (Titus's integrity = Paul's integrity) is the argument from consistency of character. Did Titus take advantage of you? Did we not act in the same spirit? Did we not take the same steps?
19: Have you been thinking all along that we have been defending (apologoumetha) ourselves to you? — the sudden shift: the entire letter is not a legal defense before the Corinthians but speech before God in Christ. It is all for your upbuilding (oikodomē), beloved.
20: For I fear (phoboumai) that perhaps when I come I may find you not as I wish — the list of vices: quarreling (eris), jealousy (zēlos), anger (thymos), selfishness (eritheiai), slander (katalalia), gossip (psithyrismos), arrogance (physiōseis), and disorder (akatastasiai) — the catalogue maps the fractures in the Corinthian community.
21: And I may have to mourn (penthēsō) over many of those who sinned earlier and have not repented of the impurity (akatharsia), sexual immorality (porneia), and sensuality (aselgeia) they have practiced — the three nouns describe the specific sins of the minority Paul fears he will find unrepentant at his third visit.
